column665: This dog is still friendly with my daughter after the incident but gives some signals of discomfort when held or squeezed by her. Most of the time it is a light crouch or lip lick. I haven’t seen any growling or bearing teeth or snapping. I think my daughter’s bite resulted from her actually hurting our dog by holding her too tightly in some way. I think she learned not to do that from that incident and although she still tries to gently hug her or kiss her head, she has not been squeezing or holding her off the ground anymore, and they both still want to be around each other. My dog approaches her with a wiggly soft body regularly and leans in for petting.

column662: The dog I am describing came from a shelter and went back due to his jealousy with the children and my husband's tension. We waited a bit, then got another big dog from a shelter. When that dog was in our home, my husband admitted to a fear of big dogs and it became clear he would not be backing down from his scary, over-dominant approach. When that dog became an escape artist, we returned him to the shelter and waited quite a while again before selecting a small breed puppy from a breeder. This dog worked out for us.
